briefing.incidentSummary:
A family noticed their internet was unusually slow for weeks.

A network scan revealed an unknown device had been connected to their home WiFi — downloading large files late at night.

The device was traced to someone nearby.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify the unauthorized device

› Trace it to its owner

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Neil V
suspects.profileNotes: Device MAC address registered to a laptop purchased under Neil V's name. Downloads averaged 4GB per night — mostly pirated movies.

suspects.name: Rohan D
suspects.profileNotes: Connected briefly while making a delivery. Only 2MB data used. No suspicious activity.

suspects.name: Preethi A
suspects.profileNotes: Has the WiFi password legitimately. Only visits on weekends. No nighttime activity.

items.label: router_access_log.txt
items.detail: Router access log from the victim's home TP-Link Archer C6. Entries show device with MAC address 44:D9:E7:2A:BC:01 first connected on 14th of last month at 00:12 AM. The device has connected every night since, consistently between midnight and 3 AM, totalling 38 sessions. The device authenticates using WPA2 with the correct pre-shared key — not a brute force. Connection signal strength averages -61 dBm, consistent with a device in an adjacent unit rather than the same household. The MAC address was not registered by the router owner as a known device. DHCP leases confirm hostname assigned: NEIL-LAPTOP.

items.label: mac_address_lookup.txt
items.detail: MAC OUI (Organisationally Unique Identifier) lookup for 44:D9:E7:2A:BC:01 returns Dell Inc. as the manufacturer. Cross-referenced against Dell's India sales database (obtained via subpoena) — the specific OUI block was assigned to a batch of Dell Inspiron 15 3520 units shipped to retail stores in this city between March and May of last year. Purchase records from the closest authorised Dell outlet show one unit from this batch sold on 12th April to a buyer named Neil V, address matches Flat 302, the adjacent unit. The MAC is not spoofed — OUI matches hardware-level assignment.

items.label: bandwidth_report.pdf
items.detail: ISP bandwidth consumption report for the victim's broadband account, covering the last 45 days. Average nightly usage between 00:00–03:00 AM: 3.8 GB per night. Daytime usage by the account owner averages 400 MB across full business hours. The night-time usage profile shows sustained high-throughput transfers consistent with torrenting or large file downloads — not casual browsing. On the three nights the external device did not connect (power outage, router restart), the 00–03 AM usage dropped to near zero. This correlation directly links the external device to the anomalous consumption.

hiddenItem.label: dhcp_hostname_log.txt
hiddenItem.detail: DHCP server lease table extracted from the router's admin panel. Lease record for MAC 44:D9:E7:2A:BC:01 shows the device hostname field as 'NEIL-LAPTOP' — the default hostname set during Windows setup, which typically uses the owner's registered name. Lease duration: 8 hours, renewed nightly. First lease issued: 14th of last month at 00:13 AM. The hostname is not configurable by the router owner — it is broadcast by the connecting device itself. Cross-referencing the name 'Neil' with residents of the building identified one match: Neil V, Flat 302, directly adjacent to the victim's unit.

minigame.title: Decode Device Hostname
minigame.hint: Caesar shift -3. What is the device's hostname?
hints: It is the name of a person followed by a device type.

onSuccess.message: dhcp_hostname_log.txt is now available in your file feed.

items.label: Signal Strength Log
items.detail: The unauthorized device consistently showed -48 dBm signal strength — indicating it was within 10 meters of the router. Flat 4B is directly adjacent.

items.label: Connection Timing
items.detail: Device connected exactly at midnight every night — suggesting an automated scheduled task, not casual browsing.

items.label: Second Unknown Device — Flat 401
items.detail: Router logs also show a second unregistered device (MAC: A8:54:B2:11:CC:04) connecting briefly on 3 occasions between 1–2 AM. This MAC resolves to an Apple iPhone, not a laptop. The device connected for under 4 minutes each time and downloaded minimal data — likely a phone checking notifications while its owner was awake. This device is from Flat 401, not Flat 302. It is not responsible for the sustained 3.8GB nightly downloads.
items.irrelevantReason: This device is from a different flat and used minimal data. The sustained high-volume theft is entirely attributable to NEIL-LAPTOP from Flat 302.

items.label: Unknown Device IP
items.detail: IP 192.168.0.109 was dynamically assigned to the unauthorized device. All large download traffic routes through this IP.

timeline.title: Family Notices Slowdown
timeline.description: Internet speed drops significantly during evening

timeline.title: Network Scan Run
timeline.description: Family runs scan, finds unknown device

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Neil V identified as the WiFi thief.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Neil V.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...Someone was stealing bandwidth. Every night. For 18 days.
They thought no one was watching.
Prove them wrong.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Router logs don't lie.
MAC addresses, signal strength, hostnames.
The device left a full trail.
aria.viewEvidence:
Every device has a fingerprint.
This one leads somewhere very close.
aria.markEvidence: Good find. Keep building the chain.
aria.decryptionHint:
The hostname is encoded.
Decode it — it might have a name in it.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
